1.1: Saul was dead. David, back from the slaughter of the Amalekites, had been at Ziklag two days.
1.2: On the third day a man came from Saul's camp. His clothes were torn; dirt was on his head—the signs of mourning. He came to David and fell prostrate before him.
1.3: 'Where do you come from?' David asked. 'I escaped from the camp of Israel,' the man said.
1.4: 'What happened?' David pressed. 'Tell me.' The man said: 'The army broke and fled. Many fell, many died. Saul is dead. Jonathan his son is dead.'
1.5: David looked at the messenger. 'How do you know Saul is dead? How do you know about Jonathan?'
1.6: The man said: 'It happened that I was on Mount Gilboa. There was Saul, leaning on his spear, and the chariots and cavalry were closing in around him.'
1.7: He looked back and saw me. He called out, and I answered: 'Here I am.'
1.8: 'Who are you?' he said. 'An Amalekite,' I told him.
1.9: 'Stand over me and finish it,' he said. 'I am in agony, but I cannot die.'
1.10: So I stood over him and killed him. After a fall like that, there was no living. I took the crown from his head and the armlet from his arm, and I have brought them to you, my lord.
1.11: David seized his robe and tore it. Every man with him did the same.
1.12: They mourned. They wept. They fasted until evening—for Saul, for Jonathan his son, for the LORD's people, for all the fallen of Israel who had died by the sword.
1.13: David turned to the messenger. 'Where are you from?' 'I am the son of a resident foreigner,' the man said. 'An Amalekite.'
1.14: David said: 'How could you not be afraid? How could you raise your hand against the LORD's anointed?'
1.15: David turned to one of his men. 'Kill him.' The man struck once. The Amalekite fell dead.
1.16: David pronounced the verdict over the body: 'Your blood is on your own head. You condemned yourself. Your own mouth said it: I killed the LORD's anointed.'
1.17: Then David sang this lament over Saul and Jonathan his son.
1.18: He ordered that the people of Judah be taught it. It stands recorded in the Book of Jashar:
1.19:
Your glory, O Israel—
slain upon the heights!
How the mighty have fallen.
1.20:
Tell it not in Gath,
proclaim it not in Ashkelon's streets—
lest the Philistine daughters rejoice,
lest those uncircumcised women exult.
1.21:
You mountains of Gilboa—
let no dew fall on you, no rain,
nor any field give of its firstfruits.
For there the shield of the mighty was thrown aside,
Saul's own shield,
as though it had never shone with oil.
1.22:
From the blood of the slain,
from the fat of the mighty—
Jonathan's bow never turned back,
Saul's sword never came home empty.
1.23:
Saul and Jonathan—
beloved and lovely in life,
in death never parted.
Swifter than eagles they were;
stronger than lions.
1.24:
O daughters of Israel, weep for Saul—
the king who clothed you in scarlet,
who adorned your garments with gold.
1.25:
How the mighty have fallen
in the midst of the battle!
Jonathan—slain upon the heights.
1.26:
I am broken for you, Jonathan, my brother.
You were so dear to me.
Your love for me was more than wonderful—
more than the love of women.
1.27:
How the mighty have fallen—
and the weapons of war are gone.

2.1: After this David sought the LORD's direction: Should he go up to one of the towns of Judah? The LORD said yes. Where? Hebron.
2.2: David went up with his two wives—Ahinoam of Jezreel and Abigail, the widow of Nabal the Carmelite.
2.3: His men came with him, each man with his family, and they settled in the towns of the Hebron region.
2.4: The men of Judah came to Hebron and anointed David king over the house of Judah. And they told him: it was the men of Jabesh-gilead who had given Saul his burial.
2.5: David sent messengers to Jabesh-gilead: 'The LORD bless you for the covenant loyalty you showed to Saul your lord by burying him.'
2.6: May the LORD now show you steadfast loyalty and faithfulness in return. And I myself will requite you for this act.'
2.7: Be strong now; take courage. Saul your lord is dead. The house of Judah has anointed me king over them.'
2.8: Meanwhile, Abner son of Ner, Saul's army commander, took Ishbosheth son of Saul and brought him across the Jordan to Mahanaim.
2.9: There he set him up as king—over Gilead, the Ashurites, Jezreel, Ephraim, Benjamin, and all Israel.
2.10: Ishbosheth was forty years old when he became king over Israel. He reigned two years. But the house of Judah followed David.
2.11: David's reign in Hebron over the house of Judah lasted seven years and six months.
2.12: Abner son of Ner marched out from Mahanaim to Gibeon, with Ishbosheth's men at his side.
2.13: Joab son of Zeruiah came out with David's men and the two forces met at the pool of Gibeon. They sat down facing each other across the water—Abner's men on one side, Joab's on the other.
2.14: Abner spoke to Joab: 'Let the young men come forward and compete before us.' Joab said, 'Let them come.'
2.15: Twelve men stepped out for Benjamin, for Ishbosheth son of Saul. Twelve men stepped out for David.
2.16: Each man seized his opponent by the head and drove his sword into his side. All twenty-four went down together. The place was called Helkath-hazzurim—Field of Sword-Edges—and it stands at Gibeon.
2.17: The battle that followed was brutal. Abner and the men of Israel were beaten by David's men.
2.18: Three sons of Zeruiah were in that battle: Joab, Abishai, and Asahel. Asahel ran like a wild gazelle.
2.19: Asahel fixed on Abner and ran—straight at him, neither right nor left.
2.20: Abner looked back. 'Asahel—is that you?' 'It is,' came the answer.
2.21: 'Break off,' Abner said. 'Pick someone else—any one of those soldiers—take his weapons.' But Asahel would not stop.
2.22: Abner called out again: 'Stop following me. Why should I kill you? How would I face your brother Joab?'
2.23: Asahel would not stop. So Abner drove the butt of his spear into his stomach, and it came out through his back. He crumpled there and died on the spot. Every man who came to that place stood still over the body.
2.24: Joab and Abishai kept up the chase. As the sun went down they came to the hill of Ammah, which overlooks Giah on the road to the Gibeon wilderness.
2.25: Benjamin's men gathered around Abner, forming a solid line on the hilltop.
2.26: Abner called out to Joab: 'Must the sword eat and eat and never stop? You know how this ends—in nothing but bitterness. How long before you call your men off their own brothers?'
2.27: Joab said: 'As God lives—if you had not spoken, my men would have chased yours until morning without stopping.'
2.28: Joab blew the ram's horn. The men stopped. The pursuit ended. The fighting was done.
2.29: Abner and his men marched through the Arabah all night. They crossed the Jordan, pushed through all of Bithron, and reached Mahanaim.
2.30: When Joab returned from the pursuit and counted his men, nineteen were missing—plus Asahel.
2.31: David's men had killed three hundred sixty of Abner's men from Benjamin.
2.32: They carried Asahel back and buried him in his father's tomb at Bethlehem. Joab and his men marched all night and were at Hebron when morning broke.

3.1: The war between Saul's house and David's house lasted a long time. David grew stronger and stronger; Saul's house grew weaker and weaker.
3.2: At Hebron David's sons were born. First was Amnon, by Ahinoam of Jezreel.
3.3: Second was Chileab, by Abigail the widow of Nabal. Third was Absalom—his mother was Maacah, daughter of Talmai king of Geshur.
3.4: Fourth was Adonijah, whose mother was Haggith. Fifth was Shephatiah by Abital.
3.5: Sixth was Ithream, by Eglah, David's wife. Six sons, born at Hebron.
3.6: During the long war between Saul's house and David's house, Abner was quietly building his own position inside Saul's camp.
3.7: Saul had a concubine named Rizpah, daughter of Aiah. Ishbosheth accused Abner: 'You have been sleeping with my father's concubine.'
3.8: Abner erupted in fury. 'Am I a dog? Some traitor to Judah? I have kept faith with your father's house all this time—with his brothers, with his friends. I have not handed you over to David. And now you accuse me over this woman!'
3.9: 'God deal with me in full if I do not do for David what the LORD swore to him—'
3.10: '—transfer the kingdom from Saul's house and raise up David's throne over all Israel and Judah, from Dan all the way to Beersheba.'
3.11: Ishbosheth had nothing to say. He was afraid of Abner.
3.12: Abner sent messengers straight to David, speaking for himself: 'Who does this land really belong to? Make a covenant with me, and I will bring all Israel over to you.'
3.13: 'Good,' David said. 'I will make a treaty with you—but on one condition: when you come to see me, bring Michal, Saul's daughter, with you.'
3.14: David also sent word to Ishbosheth: 'Return my wife Michal. I paid a hundred Philistine foreskins as the bride-price. She is mine.'
3.15: Ishbosheth had her taken from her husband, Phaltiel son of Laish.
3.16: Her husband went with her, weeping as he walked behind her all the way to Bahurim, until Abner told him: 'Go back.' And he went back.
3.17: Abner had also been working on the elders of Israel: 'You have been looking to David as king. You have wanted this for some time.'
3.18: 'Now act. The LORD himself has said: through my servant David I will rescue my people Israel from the Philistines and from every enemy.'
3.19: Abner spoke to Benjamin as well. Then he went to Hebron and laid before David in person everything that Israel and the house of Benjamin had agreed.
3.20: Abner came to Hebron with twenty men, and David held a feast for him and all who came with him.
3.21: Abner said: 'Let me go now and rally all Israel to you, my lord the king, so they can make a covenant with you and you can reign over everything you desire.' David sent Abner off, and he left in peace.
3.22: At that very moment Joab and David's men came back from a raiding expedition, carrying a large haul of plunder. Abner had just left Hebron—David had sent him away in peace.
3.23: When Joab arrived with the army, he was told: Abner son of Ner came to the king. The king received him and sent him away in peace.
3.24: Joab went straight to the king. 'What have you done? Abner came to you. Why did you let him go? He is already gone.'
3.25: 'You know what Abner is. He came here to deceive you, to scout your movements, to learn everything you are doing.'
3.26: As soon as Joab was out of David's presence, he sent messengers after Abner and brought him back from the well of Sirah. David knew nothing about it.
3.27: When Abner arrived at Hebron, Joab drew him aside in the gateway—a quiet word in private, he made it seem—and drove his knife into Abner's stomach. Abner died there: blood for blood, for Asahel.
3.28: When the news reached David, he declared publicly: 'I and my kingdom are innocent before the LORD, forever, of the blood of Abner son of Ner.'
3.29: 'Let it rest on Joab's head and on his whole family. May there never be a generation of Joab's house without someone diseased, or leprous, or dependent on others, or cut down by the sword, or without bread.'
3.30: Joab and Abishai had killed Abner. He had killed their brother Asahel at Gibeon. This was the blood-debt they paid.
3.31: David told Joab and all his men: 'Tear your clothes. Put on sackcloth. Mourn for Abner.' And David the king walked behind the bier himself.
3.32: They buried Abner in Hebron. At the grave the king wept openly, and all the people wept with him.
3.33: The king sang a lament over Abner: 'Was Abner to die like a fool?'
3.34:
Your hands were free—not bound.
Your feet were not in chains.
You fell the way one falls before ruthless men—
not on the battlefield, but by treachery.
All the people wept for him again.
3.35: All the people pressed David to eat while it was still light. But David swore: 'God deal with me severely if I touch bread or anything else before the sun goes down.'
3.36: The people noticed. They were pleased. Everything the king did in these hours had their approval.
3.37: All Israel understood that day: the king had not ordered Abner's death. The king was innocent.
3.38: The king said to his men: 'Do you not see what was lost today? A leader. A great man in Israel.'
3.39: 'I was anointed king—and yet I am weak. These sons of Zeruiah are too hard for me. May the LORD repay the one who did this evil according to what he has done.'

4.1: When Ishbosheth heard that Abner was dead, he lost his nerve. All Israel was shaken.
4.2: Ishbosheth had two commanders of raiding bands: Rechab and Baanah, sons of Rimmon the Beerothite, from the tribe of Benjamin. Beeroth was counted as part of Benjamin's territory.
4.3: The Beerothites had earlier fled to Gittaim, where they lived as resident aliens—and still do.
4.4: Jonathan son of Saul had a son named Mephibosheth who was lame in both feet. He was five years old when the news of Saul's and Jonathan's deaths reached Jezreel. In the panic, his nurse scooped him up and ran—and he fell and was crippled as she fled.
4.5: Rechab and Baanah, the sons of Rimmon the Beerothite, went to Ishbosheth's house at midday while the king was resting in the heat of the day.
4.6: They entered the house as if to collect wheat and stabbed him in the stomach. Then they ran.
4.7: Ishbosheth had been lying on his bed in his bedroom. They struck him, killed him, and cut off his head. They took the head and moved through the Arabah all night.
4.8: They brought Ishbosheth's head to David at Hebron. 'Here is the head of Ishbosheth son of Saul—your enemy, the man who wanted you dead. The LORD has given our lord the king justice today against Saul and his line.'
4.9: David answered Rechab and Baanah—the sons of Rimmon the Beerothite—and said: 'As the LORD lives, who has rescued my life from every danger—'
4.10: '—when a man came to me at Ziklag claiming Saul was dead, thinking to bring me good news, I killed him. That was the reward I gave for that news.'
4.11: 'Now you have murdered an innocent man in his own house while he slept. How much more certainly will I require his blood from your hands and remove you from this earth?'
4.12: David gave the order. His men killed them—cutting off their hands and feet and hanging the bodies beside the pool at Hebron. The head of Ishbosheth they buried in Abner's tomb at Hebron.

5.1: Then all the tribes of Israel came to David at Hebron. 'We are your own flesh and blood,' they said.
5.2: 'Even in Saul's time it was you who led Israel out and brought them back. And the LORD told you: You shall shepherd my people Israel; you shall be the ruler over Israel.'
5.3: All the elders of Israel came to the king at Hebron. King David made a covenant with them before the LORD, and they anointed David king over all Israel.
5.4: David was thirty years old when he became king. He reigned forty years in all.
5.5: Seven years and six months at Hebron over Judah. Thirty-three years at Jerusalem over all Israel and Judah.
5.6: David and his men marched to Jerusalem against the Jebusites, the people of the land. 'You will never get in here,' they called out. 'Even the blind and the lame could stop you.' They were taunting David—saying the city was impregnable.
5.7: David captured it anyway—the stronghold of Zion, the city of David.
5.8: David said that day: 'Whoever strikes the Jebusites must go up through the water shaft—go after the lame and the blind who mock us.' From that day the saying was made: 'The blind and the lame shall not enter the house.'
5.9: David settled in the stronghold and named it the city of David. He built it up all around from the Millo inward.
5.10: And David grew greater and greater, because the LORD God of hosts was with him.
5.11: Hiram king of Tyre sent envoys to David, with cedar timber and carpenters and stonemasons who built him a royal palace.
5.12: David understood: the LORD had established him as king over Israel, had elevated his kingdom—not for David's own glory but for the sake of Israel, the LORD's people.
5.13: After moving to Jerusalem, David took additional concubines and wives, and more sons and daughters were born to him.
5.14: These are the children born to him in Jerusalem: Shammua, Shobab, Nathan, Solomon,
5.17: When the Philistines heard that David had been anointed king over all Israel, they came up in full force to hunt him down. David heard the news and went down to the stronghold.
5.18: The Philistines had spread out in the Valley of Rephaim.
5.19: David asked the LORD: 'Shall I attack the Philistines? Will you give them to me?' The LORD answered: 'Go. I will certainly give the Philistines into your hand.'
5.20: David came to Baal-perazim and struck the Philistines there. He said: 'The LORD has burst through my enemies before me like a flood breaking through.' So the place was named Baal-perazim—Lord of the Breakthrough.
5.21: The Philistines fled, leaving their idols behind. David and his men burned them.
5.22: The Philistines came back and spread out in the Valley of Rephaim again.
5.23: David again asked the LORD for direction. This time: 'Do not attack them head-on. Circle around behind and come at them from the side, opposite the balsam trees.'
5.24: 'When you hear the sound of movement in the tops of the balsam trees, that is your signal—the LORD has gone out ahead of you to strike the Philistine army.'
5.25: David obeyed, and struck the Philistines from Geba to Gezer.

6.1: David mustered thirty thousand picked men of Israel.
6.2: David and all the people with him went to Baale-judah to bring up the ark of God—the ark that bears the name of the LORD of hosts, who sits enthroned above the cherubim.
6.3: They loaded the ark of God onto a new cart and brought it out from the house of Abinadab on the hill. Uzzah and Ahio, Abinadab's sons, guided the cart.
6.4: Ahio walked ahead of the ark.
6.5: David and all Israel celebrated before the LORD with every kind of musical instrument—lyres, harps, tambourines, castanets, and cymbals.
6.6: At the threshing floor of Nacon the oxen stumbled. Uzzah reached out and took hold of the ark of God to steady it.
6.7: The LORD's anger flared. God struck Uzzah down on the spot for his presumptuousness, and he died there beside the ark.
6.8: David was shaken and angry—the LORD had burst out against Uzzah. The place was named Perez-uzzah, and it keeps that name to this day.
6.9: David was afraid of the LORD that day. 'How can the ark of the LORD come to me now?' he said.
6.10: David would not bring the ark into the city of David. Instead he turned aside and left it at the house of Obed-edom the Gittite.
6.11: The ark stayed at Obed-edom's house three months. The LORD blessed Obed-edom and all who were in his household.
6.12: News reached King David: the LORD was blessing Obed-edom's household and everything he owned, because of the ark. So David went and brought the ark of God up from Obed-edom's house to the city of David with great joy.
6.13: Six paces. After the ark-bearers had taken six steps, David sacrificed an ox and a fatted calf.
6.14: David danced before the LORD with everything in him, wearing a linen ephod.
6.15: David and all Israel brought the ark of the LORD up with shouts and the blast of the ram's horn.
6.16: As the ark of the LORD entered the city of David, Michal daughter of Saul looked out from a window. She saw King David leaping and dancing before the LORD—and she despised him in her heart.
6.17: They brought the ark in and set it in its place inside the tent David had pitched for it. David offered burnt offerings and peace offerings before the LORD.
6.18: When David had finished the offerings, he blessed the people in the name of the LORD of hosts.
6.19: He distributed food to the whole multitude of Israel, men and women together—bread, meat, and a raisin cake for each. Then everyone went home.
6.20: David came home to bless his household. But Michal daughter of Saul came out to meet him. 'What a glorious display the king of Israel made today,' she said, 'stripping himself in front of his servants' slave girls like some worthless nobody.'
6.21: David answered Michal: 'It was before the LORD—who chose me over your father and his whole house to make me ruler over Israel, the LORD's people. It is before the LORD that I celebrate.'
6.22: 'I will make myself yet more undignified than this, and be lowly in my own eyes. But those slave girls you despise? They will honor me.'
6.23: Michal daughter of Saul had no child to the day of her death.

7.1: When David was settled in his palace and the LORD had given him rest from all his enemies on every side,
7.2: David said to Nathan the prophet: 'Here I am, living in a cedar palace—while the ark of God sits in a tent.'
7.3: Nathan said: 'Do whatever is in your heart, for the LORD is with you.'
7.4: But that night the word of the LORD came to Nathan:
7.5: 'Go, tell my servant David: This is what the LORD says—Is it you who will build me a house to live in?'
7.6: 'From the day I brought Israel out of Egypt until now, I have never lived in a house. I have moved about with a tent as my dwelling.'
7.7: 'In all the places I traveled with Israel, did I ever say a single word to any tribal leader I had appointed to shepherd my people? Did I ever ask: Why have you not built me a house of cedar?'
7.8: 'Now say this to my servant David: This is what the LORD of hosts says—I took you from the pasture, from behind the flock, and made you ruler over my people Israel.'
7.9: 'I was with you everywhere you went. I cut off all your enemies before you. And I gave you a name as great as the greatest names on earth.'
7.10: 'I will appoint a place for my people Israel and will plant them there to live undisturbed. Never again will the wicked oppress them as they have done before.'
7.11: '—as they have done since the time I appointed judges over Israel. I will give you rest from all your enemies. And now the LORD declares this to you: the LORD himself will build you a house.'
7.12: 'When your days are done and you lie down with your fathers, I will raise up your offspring after you—one born from your own body—and I will establish his kingdom.'
7.13: 'He will build a house for my name, and I will establish his kingdom's throne forever.'
7.14: 'I will be his father, and he will be my son. When he goes wrong, I will correct him with the rod that fathers use on sons—but I will not abandon him.'
7.15: 'My covenant faithfulness will not be withdrawn from him—as I withdrew it from Saul, whom I removed before you.'
7.16: 'Your house and your kingdom will stand before me forever. Your throne will be established forever.'
7.17: Nathan told David every word of this, and recounted the whole vision.
7.18: David went in and sat before the LORD and said: 'Who am I, Lord God? What is my family, that you have brought me this far?'
7.19: 'And still this was a small thing in your eyes, Lord God—so you have spoken about your servant's house for ages to come. This is the charter you have set for all humanity, Lord God.'
7.20: 'What more can David say to you? You know your servant, Lord God.'
7.21: 'For your servant's sake, and out of your own heart's purpose, you have done all these great things and let your servant know them.'
7.22: 'Therefore you are great, LORD God. There is none like you—no God beside you—as everything we have ever heard with our ears declares.'
7.23: 'And who is like your people Israel—the one nation on earth for whom God himself went out to redeem a people, making a name for himself, doing great and awesome things by driving out nations and their gods before the people he redeemed from Egypt?'
7.24: 'You have established Israel as your own people forever. And you, LORD, have become their God.'
7.25: 'And now, LORD God, let the word you have spoken about your servant and his house stand forever. Do as you have promised.'
7.26: 'May your name be magnified forever, so that all will say: The LORD of hosts is the God of Israel. And let the house of your servant David stand firm before you.'
7.27: 'For it is you, LORD of hosts, God of Israel, who revealed it to your servant: I will build you a house. That is why your servant has found the heart to pray this prayer before you.'
7.28: 'Now, Lord God, you are God. Your words are true. You have made this gracious promise to your servant.'
7.29: 'Therefore may it please you to bless the house of your servant, that it may stand before you forever. For you, Lord God, have spoken—and by your blessing the house of your servant will be blessed forever.'

8.1: After this David struck the Philistines and subdued them, taking Metheg-ammah from their grip.
8.2: He struck down Moab and measured the prisoners with a cord, making them lie on the ground. Two-thirds he measured out for death; one-third he kept alive. Moab became David's subjects, bringing tribute.
8.3: David also struck down Hadadezer son of Rehob, king of Zobah, who was marching to restore his power at the Euphrates River.
8.4: David captured 1,700 horsemen and 20,000 foot soldiers. He hamstrung the chariot horses, keeping only enough for a hundred chariots.
8.5: When the Syrians of Damascus came to reinforce Hadadezer, David cut them down—twenty-two thousand men.
8.6: David garrisoned Aram of Damascus, and the Syrians became his subjects, paying tribute. The LORD gave David victory wherever he went.
8.7: David took the gold shields that Hadadezer's officers had carried and brought them to Jerusalem.
8.8: From Betah and Berothai—cities of Hadadezer—David carried off a great quantity of bronze.
8.9: Toi king of Hamath heard that David had shattered the entire army of Hadadezer.
8.10: Toi sent his son Joram to King David to greet him and bless him for defeating Hadadezer—Toi and Hadadezer had long been at war. Joram brought silver, gold, and bronze as gifts.
8.11: King David dedicated all of this to the LORD—the silver and gold from every nation he had subdued:
8.12: from Edom and Moab, from Ammon and the Philistines and Amalek, and from the spoil of Hadadezer son of Rehob, king of Zobah.
8.13: David made a great name for himself when he returned from striking down eighteen thousand in the Valley of Salt.
8.14: He placed garrisons throughout Edom, and all the Edomites became David's subjects. The LORD gave David victory wherever he went.
8.15: David reigned over all Israel and executed justice and righteousness for all his people.
8.16: Joab son of Zeruiah commanded the army. Jehoshaphat son of Ahilud was the recorder.
8.17: Zadok son of Ahitub and Ahimelech son of Abiathar served as priests. Seraiah was the secretary.
8.18: Benaiah son of Jehoiada was over the Cherethites and the Pelethites. David's sons were royal advisers.

9.1: David asked: 'Is there anyone still left from the house of Saul to whom I can show covenant loyalty for Jonathan's sake?'
9.2: There was a man named Ziba who had been a servant of Saul's household. They brought him to David. 'Are you Ziba?' the king asked. 'I am your servant,' he said.
9.3: 'Is there no one left from Saul's house,' the king asked, 'to whom I can show the covenant loyalty of God?' Ziba said: 'Jonathan has a son—he is lame in both feet.'
9.4: 'Where is he?' David asked. 'In Lo-debar,' said Ziba. 'At the house of Machir son of Ammiel.'
9.5: King David sent for him and had him brought from the house of Machir son of Ammiel in Lo-debar.
9.6: Mephibosheth son of Jonathan son of Saul came to David and fell to the ground in homage. David said: 'Mephibosheth.' 'Your servant,' he answered.
9.7: David said: 'Do not be afraid. I will show you covenant loyalty for your father Jonathan's sake. I will restore all the land of your grandfather Saul to you, and you will eat at my table—always.'
9.8: He bowed down and said: 'Who is your servant, that you should regard a dead dog like me?'
9.9: The king summoned Ziba, Saul's servant, and said: 'Everything that belonged to Saul and all his house I have now given to your master's grandson.'
9.10: 'You and your sons and servants will farm the land for him and bring in the harvest—that is how Mephibosheth your master's grandson will be provided for. But Mephibosheth will eat at my table.' Ziba had fifteen sons and twenty servants.
9.11: Ziba said: 'Your servant will do whatever my lord the king commands.' So Mephibosheth ate at David's table—treated as one of the king's own sons.
9.12: Mephibosheth had a young son named Micha. Everyone in Ziba's household became servants to Mephibosheth.
9.13: Mephibosheth lived in Jerusalem. He ate at the king's table, always. He was lame in both feet.

10.1: After this the king of Ammon died, and his son Hanun became king.
10.2: David said: 'I will show covenant loyalty to Hanun son of Nahash, as his father showed it to me.' He sent his servants to Hanun to offer condolences over his father. David's men came to the land of Ammon.
10.3: But the Ammonite commanders said to Hanun their lord: 'Do you think David sent these men to honor your father? Has he not sent them to survey the city—to spy it out and bring it down?'
10.4: So Hanun seized David's servants and humiliated them: he shaved off half of each man's beard and cut their garments at the waist, leaving them exposed, and sent them away.
10.5: David was told. He sent men to meet them on the road, because the men were deeply shamed. 'Stay at Jericho,' the king said, 'until your beards grow back. Then come home.'
10.6: The Ammonites knew they had made themselves enemies of David, so they hired mercenaries: the Syrians of Beth-rehob and Zobah—twenty thousand foot soldiers—plus the king of Maacah with a thousand men, and the men of Tob with twelve thousand.
10.7: When David heard, he sent Joab with the full army of fighting men.
10.8: The Ammonites came out and drew up at the city gate. The Syrians of Zobah, Rehob, Tob, and Maacah took position separately in the open field—a two-front threat.
10.9: Joab saw that the attack was coming from two directions—front and rear. He chose the best of Israel's soldiers and arrayed them against the Syrians.
10.10: The rest he put under his brother Abishai, who faced the Ammonites.
10.11: 'If the Syrians are too strong for me, come to my aid. If the Ammonites are too strong for you, I will come to yours.'
10.12: 'Be strong. Fight for our people and for the cities of our God. The LORD will do what is right.'
10.13: Joab advanced against the Syrians, and they fled.
10.14: When the Ammonites saw the Syrians had fled, they too fled before Abishai and retreated into the city. Joab returned to Jerusalem.
10.15: The Syrians saw they had been broken before Israel. They regrouped.
10.16: Hadadezer sent for the Syrians beyond the Euphrates. They came to Helam under Shobach, the commander of Hadadezer's army.
10.17: David was told. He gathered all Israel, crossed the Jordan, and came to Helam. The Syrians drew up against him and gave battle.
10.18: The Syrians fled before Israel. David killed seven hundred chariot drivers and forty thousand horsemen, and Shobach the army commander died there.
10.19: All the vassal kings of Hadadezer, seeing they were beaten, made peace with Israel and submitted to them. After that, the Syrians were unwilling to help Ammon again.

11.1: In the spring, the season when kings go to war, David sent Joab and his officers and all Israel. They laid waste to Ammon and besieged Rabbah. But David stayed in Jerusalem.
11.2: Late one afternoon David rose from his couch and walked on the palace roof. From the roof he saw a woman bathing. She was very beautiful.
11.3: David sent to find out who she was. Someone said: 'Is that not Bathsheba, daughter of Eliam—the wife of Uriah the Hittite?'
11.4: David sent messengers and took her. She came to him, and he lay with her. She had just completed her purification. Then she went back home.
11.5: The woman conceived. She sent word to David: 'I am pregnant.'
11.6: David sent word to Joab: 'Send me Uriah the Hittite.' Joab sent him.
11.7: When Uriah came, David asked after Joab, after the men, after the progress of the war.
11.8: David said: 'Go home. Rest.' Uriah left the palace, and a gift from the king went with him.
11.9: But Uriah slept at the palace entrance with all the king's servants. He did not go home.
11.10: When David was told, he summoned Uriah. 'You just got back from a journey. Why didn't you go home?'
11.11: Uriah said: 'The ark of God is in a tent. Israel and Judah are in tents. Joab and all your servants are camped in the open field. And I am to go home and eat and drink and sleep with my wife? As you live—as your life itself is my witness—I will not do it.'
11.12: 'Stay today,' David said. 'I'll send you back tomorrow.' So Uriah stayed in Jerusalem that day and the next.
11.13: David invited him to dinner. Uriah ate and drank—David plied him with wine until he was drunk. That evening Uriah went out and lay down with the king's servants. He did not go home.
11.14: In the morning David wrote a letter to Joab and sent it by Uriah's own hand.
11.15: The letter said: 'Place Uriah in the front line where the battle is hardest. Then pull back—so that he is cut down and dies.'
11.16: Joab, watching the city, knew where the toughest defenders were posted. He put Uriah there.
11.17: The city's defenders came out and attacked. Some of David's men fell. Uriah the Hittite also died.
11.18: Joab sent David a full report on the fighting.
11.19: He instructed the messenger: 'When you have finished reporting the battle to the king,'
11.20: 'if the king grows angry and asks why you pressed so close to the wall—did you not know they would shoot from there?—'
11.21: '—who killed Abimelech son of Jerubbesheth? A woman dropped a millstone on him from the wall at Thebez. That is how he died. Why did you go near the wall?—then say: Your servant Uriah the Hittite is dead.'
11.22: The messenger went and came and told David everything Joab had sent him to say.
11.23: The messenger said: 'The men came out strong against us into the field, but we drove them back to the city gate.'
11.24: 'The archers shot from the wall and some of your men died. Your servant Uriah the Hittite is also dead.'
11.25: David told the messenger: 'Say this to Joab: Do not be troubled by this. The sword cuts down one man as well as another. Press the attack. Take the city. Tell him to be strong.'
11.26: When Uriah's wife heard that her husband was dead, she mourned for him.
11.27: When the mourning was done, David sent for her. She came to his house. She became his wife and bore him a son. But what David had done was evil in the sight of the LORD.

12.1: The LORD sent Nathan to David. Nathan came and said: 'There were two men in a city—one rich, one poor.'
12.2: 'The rich man had enormous flocks and herds.'
12.3: 'The poor man had nothing—one little ewe lamb he had bought. He raised it. It grew up with him and his children, ate from his plate, drank from his cup, slept in his arms. It was like a daughter to him.'
12.4: 'A traveler came to the rich man. The rich man would not take from his own flock. He took the poor man's lamb—and served it to his guest.'
12.5: David's anger blazed. He said to Nathan: 'As the LORD lives, the man who did this deserves to die!'
12.6: 'He must pay back fourfold—because he had no pity.'
12.7: Nathan said: 'You are the man. This is what the LORD God of Israel says: I anointed you king over Israel. I rescued you from Saul's hand.'
12.8: 'I gave you your master's house. I placed his wives in your care. I gave you Israel and Judah. If that had been too little, I would have given you still more.'
12.9: 'Why have you despised the word of the LORD and done this evil? You killed Uriah the Hittite with the sword. You took his wife. You killed him with the sword of Ammon.'
12.10: 'Therefore the sword will never leave your house. Because you despised me and took the wife of Uriah the Hittite.'
12.11: 'This is what the LORD says: I will raise up disaster against you from within your own house. I will take your wives before your eyes and give them to another man, and he will lie with them in the open light of day.'
12.12: 'You acted in secret. I will do this in front of all Israel, in the full light of the sun.'
12.13: David said to Nathan: 'I have sinned against the LORD.' Nathan said: 'The LORD has taken away your sin. You will not die.'
12.14: 'Nevertheless, by this deed you have given the LORD's enemies reason to despise his name. The child who has been born to you will die.'
12.15: Nathan went home. The LORD struck the child that Uriah's wife had borne to David, and the child fell sick.
12.16: David pleaded with God for the child. He fasted. He went in and lay on the ground all night.
12.17: The elders of his household stood over him and tried to lift him. He would not get up. He would not eat.
12.18: On the seventh day the child died. David's servants were afraid to tell him. They said: 'While the child was still alive, he wouldn't listen to us. How can we tell him the child is dead? He might do something terrible.'
12.19: But David saw his servants whispering. He understood. 'Is the child dead?' he asked. 'He is dead,' they said.
12.20: David rose from the ground. He washed, anointed himself, changed his clothes, and went into the house of the LORD and worshiped. Then he came home, asked for food, and ate.
12.21: His servants said: 'We don't understand. While the child was alive, you fasted and wept. Now that he is dead, you rise and eat?'
12.22: He said: 'While the child was alive, I fasted and wept. I thought: Who knows? Perhaps the LORD will show me grace and the child will live.'
12.23: 'But he is dead. Why should I fast? Can I bring him back? I will go to him one day. He will not come back to me.'
12.24: David comforted Bathsheba his wife. He went in to her, and she bore a son. David named him Solomon. And the LORD loved him.
12.25: Through the prophet Nathan the LORD sent word, and the child was also called Jedidiah—Beloved of the LORD—because the LORD loved him.
12.26: Meanwhile, Joab was fighting Rabbah of Ammon and had taken the royal quarter of the city.
12.27: Joab sent word to David: 'I have been fighting Rabbah. I have taken the water district.'
12.28: 'Now gather the rest of the forces, come and take the city yourself—before I take it and it bears my name, not yours.'
12.29: David mustered the army, marched to Rabbah, and took it.
12.30: He took the crown from the head of the Ammonite king—a talent of gold set with precious stones—and it was placed on David's head. He brought out an enormous amount of plunder from the city.
12.31: He brought the people out and put them to work—under saws, iron picks, and axes, and at the brick kilns. He did this to all the cities of Ammon. Then David and all the army returned to Jerusalem.

13.1: After this, David's son Absalom had a beautiful sister named Tamar. And David's son Amnon became obsessed with her.
13.2: Amnon was consumed with longing for Tamar—consumed to the point of sickness. She was a virgin, protected by the court; there was nothing he could do. Or so it seemed.
13.3: But Amnon had a friend: Jonadab son of Shimeah, David's brother. Jonadab was clever—dangerously clever.
13.4: Jonadab said to him, 'Why are you getting thinner day by day, prince? Tell me.' Amnon said: 'I am in love with Tamar—my brother Absalom's sister.'
13.5: Jonadab had a plan. 'Take to your bed. Pretend you are sick. When your father comes, say to him: Let my sister Tamar come and prepare food for me—in my sight, so I can watch her, and eat from her hand.'
13.6: Amnon did it. He took to his bed and played sick. When David came to visit, Amnon said: 'Please let my sister Tamar come and bake a few cakes for me in my room. I want to eat from her own hand.'
13.7: David sent a message to Tamar at home: 'Go to your brother Amnon's house and prepare food for him.' He had no idea.
13.8: Tamar went to Amnon's house. He was lying in bed. She took the dough, kneaded it, and shaped the cakes before his eyes, then put them in the fire.
13.9: She took the pan and served him. He refused to eat. 'Everyone out,' Amnon said. They all went.
13.10: Then Amnon said to Tamar: 'Bring the food into the inner room. I want to eat from your hand.' Tamar took the cakes she had made and brought them to him in the inner room.
13.11: As she brought the food close, he seized her. 'Come to bed with me, my sister,' he said.
13.12: 'No, my brother!' she said. 'Do not force me. This is not done in Israel—this is a vile disgrace. Do not do this thing.'
13.13: 'Where would I go with my shame? And you—you would become one of the fools who disgrace Israel. Speak to the king. He will not refuse you.'
13.14: He would not listen. He was stronger than she was. He raped her.
13.15: Then Amnon hated her—violently, suddenly. The hatred that came over him was greater than the obsession that had consumed him. 'Get up,' he said. 'Get out.'
13.16: 'No,' she said. 'Sending me away like this is even worse than what you just did.' But he would not listen.
13.17: He called his servant. 'Get this woman out of my sight. Bolt the door after her.'
13.18: She was wearing the ornamented robe worn by the royal virgin daughters. The servant put her out and bolted the door.
13.19: Tamar put ashes on her head. She tore the ornamented robe. She pressed her hand to her head and walked, weeping loudly, out into the open.
13.20: Absalom saw her and knew. 'Was it Amnon?' he said. 'Be quiet now, sister. He is your brother. Do not dwell on it.' So Tamar lived in Absalom's house—ruined, silent, alone.
13.21: When King David heard what had happened, he was furious. And he did nothing.
13.22: Absalom said nothing to Amnon—not a word, good or bad. But he hated him. Amnon had raped Tamar his sister. Absalom remembered.
13.23: Two years passed. Absalom held a shearing feast at Baal-hazor, near Ephraim, and invited all the king's sons.
13.24: Absalom came to the king. 'Your servant is holding a shearing. Come with me—you and your men.'
13.25: David said, 'No, my son—we would all be a burden to you.' Absalom pressed him. David would not go. He gave him his blessing.
13.26: 'Then at least let my brother Amnon go with us.' David asked, 'Why should Amnon go?'
13.27: Absalom pressed until David agreed. Amnon went—and all the king's sons with him.
13.28: Absalom had instructed his men beforehand: 'When Amnon is drunk—when I give the word, Strike Amnon—kill him. Do not hesitate. I take full responsibility. Be brave and do it.'
13.29: Absalom's men struck Amnon dead, exactly as ordered. Every one of the king's sons leapt up, got on his mule, and fled.
13.30: While they were still on the road, the news reached David: 'Absalom has killed all your sons. Not one is left.'
13.31: The king stood up, tore his robes, and threw himself to the ground. All his servants stood there, their robes torn.
13.32: Then Jonadab son of Shimeah—David's brother—spoke. 'My lord must not think all the princes are dead. Only Amnon is dead. Absalom has been planning this since the day Amnon raped Tamar his sister. He has been waiting two years for this moment.'
13.33: 'Do not grieve for all of them, my lord. Only Amnon is dead.'
13.34: Absalom had fled. The lookout raised his eyes: a great crowd was coming down the road along the hillside.
13.35: Jonadab said: 'Look—the king's sons are coming. It is as I said.'
13.36: He had barely finished speaking when the princes came in and broke into weeping. The king and all his men wept with them—loud, uncontrolled grief.
13.37: Absalom had fled to Talmai son of Ammihud, king of Geshur—his mother's people. And David mourned for his son, day after day.
13.38: Absalom lived in Geshur three years.
13.39: The king's longing to go to Absalom grew unbearable. The grief for Amnon had run its course—Amnon was gone. But Absalom was out there, unreachable.

14.1: Joab son of Zeruiah read the king's mind: David's heart had gone to Absalom.
14.2: Joab sent to Tekoa and brought a wise woman from there. He told her: 'Play the part of a woman in mourning—wear mourning clothes, no oil, the look of someone who has grieved a long time.'
14.3: 'Go to the king and say what I tell you.' He coached her word by word.
14.4: The woman of Tekoa came before the king. She fell to the ground in obeisance and cried: 'Help me, O king!'
14.5: 'What troubles you?' the king asked. 'I am a widow,' she said. 'My husband is dead.'
14.6: 'I had two sons. They quarreled in the field—no one was there to stop them—and one killed the other.'
14.7: 'Now the whole family has turned on me. Give us the killer, they say—give him over so we can execute him for his brother's blood, even if it destroys the heir. They want to snuff out the one coal I have left—leave my husband with no name, no trace, anywhere on this earth.'
14.8: The king said: 'Go home. I will give instructions on your behalf.'
14.9: 'My lord,' the woman said, 'let any guilt fall on me and on my father's house. The king and his throne must be kept clean.'
14.10: 'If anyone threatens you further,' the king said, 'bring him to me. He will not lay a hand on you.'
14.11: 'Please,' she said, 'let the king swear by the LORD your God—so that the blood-avenger does not destroy my son.' He said: 'As the LORD lives, not a hair of your son shall touch the ground.'
14.12: The woman said: 'May your servant say one more word to my lord the king?' 'Say it,' he said.
14.13: 'Then why have you done the same thing to God's own people? By making this ruling, you have convicted yourself—you, the king, will not bring home the one you have banished.'
14.14: 'We all die. We are like water poured on the ground—it cannot be gathered back. But God does not sweep away a life; he finds a way to bring the outcast home. He does not want anyone permanently cut off from his presence.'
14.15: 'I have come to say this because the people have frightened me. I thought: I will speak to the king. Perhaps he will do what his servant asks.'
14.16: 'The king will hear and rescue his servant from the hand of those who would cut off both me and my son from God's inheritance.'
14.17: 'I thought: the word of my lord the king will give me rest—for he is like an angel of God in knowing good from evil. May the LORD your God be with you.'
14.18: The king said to her: 'Answer me one thing. Hide nothing.' 'Let my lord speak,' she said.
14.19: The king said: 'Was Joab behind this?' The woman said: 'As you live, my lord, no one can turn right or left from a word my lord has spoken. Yes—it was Joab who sent me. He put every one of these words in my mouth.'
14.20: 'He did it to change the direction of things. But my lord has wisdom like the wisdom of an angel of God—there is nothing happening in this land that escapes him.'
14.21: The king said to Joab: 'I have made my decision. Go—bring Absalom back.'
14.22: Joab fell with his face to the ground in obeisance and blessed the king. 'Today your servant knows he has found favor in your sight, my lord,' Joab said. 'You have done what I asked.'
14.23: Joab went to Geshur and brought Absalom back to Jerusalem.
14.24: 'Let him go to his own house,' the king said. 'He is not to see my face.' So Absalom came home to Jerusalem—but remained cut off from his father.
14.25: In all Israel there was no man as celebrated for his looks as Absalom. From his feet to his head there was not a single flaw.
14.26: He cut his hair once a year—it grew too heavy. When he weighed it, it came to two hundred shekels by the royal standard. Even his hair was extraordinary.
14.27: Absalom had three sons and a daughter. The daughter was named Tamar—a beautiful woman, named for her violated aunt.
14.28: Two full years Absalom lived in Jerusalem without being allowed into his father's presence.
14.29: Absalom sent for Joab—he wanted Joab to take a message to the king. Joab would not come. He sent again. Joab would not come.
14.30: Absalom told his servants: 'Joab's field is right next to mine. There is barley in it. Burn it.' They burned it.
14.31: Joab came to Absalom's house. 'Why did your men burn my field?'
14.32: 'I sent for you,' Absalom said. 'I wanted you to go to the king and ask: why did I come back from Geshur? I would have been better off staying there. Now let me see the king's face. If I am guilty, let him kill me.'
14.33: Joab went and reported to the king. David summoned Absalom. Absalom came and bowed with his face to the ground. The king kissed him.

15.1: After this Absalom got himself a chariot and horses, with fifty men to run ahead of him. The performance of kingship.
15.2: He would rise early and stand at the gate road. Whenever someone came to the king for a legal judgment, Absalom would call out: 'Where are you from?' 'Your servant is from one of the tribes of Israel,' the man would say.
15.3: Absalom would say: 'Your claim is just and right. But there is no one appointed by the king to hear it.'
15.4: 'If only I were judge in this land,' Absalom would say. 'Anyone with a case could come to me. I would give him justice.'
15.5: When a man would bow before him, Absalom would reach out, pull him close, and kiss him.
15.6: This was his pattern with every Israelite who came for justice before the king. And so Absalom stole the hearts of Israel.
15.7: Four years into this, Absalom said to the king: 'Let me go to Hebron and keep the vow I made to the LORD.'
15.8: 'When I was in Geshur, I made a vow: if the LORD brings me back to Jerusalem, I will worship him in Hebron.' A lie dressed as devotion.
15.9: The king said: 'Go in peace.' Absalom rose and went to Hebron.
15.10: But Absalom had already sent agents throughout every tribe of Israel with this message: 'When you hear the ram's horn, shout: Absalom reigns in Hebron!'
15.11: Two hundred men from Jerusalem went with him—invited guests, completely unsuspecting. They knew nothing of the plan.
15.12: Absalom had sent for Ahithophel the Gilonite—David's own counselor—calling him from Giloh while the sacrificial smoke was still rising. The conspiracy was gaining strength. The people kept coming over to Absalom.
15.13: A messenger reached David: 'The hearts of Israel have gone after Absalom.'
15.14: David said to all his servants in Jerusalem: 'Get up. We flee—now. There is no escaping Absalom if we stay. He will overwhelm us, and the city will be put to the sword.'
15.15: His servants said: 'Whatever our lord the king decides, we are ready.'
15.16: The king walked out, his whole household after him. He left ten concubines to keep the palace.
15.17: The king went out, all the people streaming after him. They stopped at the last house at the city's edge.
15.18: His servants marched past—the Cherethites, the Pelethites, and all six hundred Gittites who had followed him from Gath. Every one of them.
15.19: The king stopped Ittai the Gittite. 'Why are you coming with us? Go back to whoever is king now. You are a foreigner—an exile from your own land.'
15.20: 'You only arrived yesterday. Should I drag you into this uncertainty with me? Go back. Take your people with you. May the LORD show you covenant loyalty and faithfulness.'
15.21: Ittai answered: 'As the LORD lives and as my lord the king lives—wherever you are, in life or in death, I will be there too.'
15.22: David said: 'Then march.' And Ittai the Gittite marched—his men, the families, everyone with him.
15.23: The whole countryside wept aloud as the people passed. The king crossed the Kidron. Everyone moved toward the wilderness.
15.24: Zadok was there with all the Levites, carrying the ark of the covenant of God. They set the ark down and waited until all the people had come out of the city. Abiathar came up as well.
15.25: The king said to Zadok: 'Bring the ark back into the city. If the LORD favors me, he will bring me back and let me see the ark and his dwelling again.'
15.26: 'But if he says he wants none of me—then here I am. Let him do what is right in his eyes.'
15.27: The king said to Zadok: 'Return to the city in safety—you and Abiathar, and your sons with you: Ahimaaz your son and Jonathan son of Abiathar.'
15.28: 'I will wait at the ford in the wilderness until you send me word.'
15.29: Zadok and Abiathar took the ark back into Jerusalem and stayed.
15.30: David went up the Mount of Olives weeping as he climbed. His head was covered; his feet were bare. Every person with him had their head covered, weeping as they went.
15.31: David was told: Ahithophel is among the conspirators. David prayed: 'O LORD—make Ahithophel's counsel into foolishness.'
15.32: When David reached the top—the place of worship—Hushai the Archite was there to meet him, his robe torn and dirt on his head.
15.33: David said to him: 'If you come with me, you will be a burden.'
15.34: 'But if you go back into the city and tell Absalom: I am your servant, O king—as I served your father, so I will serve you—then you can work against Ahithophel from the inside.'
15.35: 'Zadok and Abiathar the priests are there—whatever you hear from the palace, tell them.'
15.36: 'Ahimaaz son of Zadok and Jonathan son of Abiathar—their two sons are there. Use them to pass everything on to me.'
15.37: Hushai, David's friend, went back into the city. He arrived just as Absalom was entering Jerusalem.

16.1: Just past the summit, Ziba the servant of Mephibosheth met David. Two donkeys, saddled, loaded: two hundred loaves of bread, a hundred clusters of raisins, a hundred summer figs, a skin of wine.
16.2: The king asked: 'What is all this?' Ziba said: 'The donkeys for the royal household to ride. The bread and fruit for the men. The wine for anyone who collapses in the wilderness.'
16.3: The king asked: 'Where is your master's son?' Ziba said: 'He stayed in Jerusalem. He said: Today Israel will restore my grandfather's kingdom to me.'
16.4: 'Everything that belonged to Mephibosheth is yours,' the king said. 'I bow before you,' Ziba said. 'May I find favor in your sight, my lord.'
16.5: As David came to Bahurim, a man came out of the town—Shimei son of Gera, from Saul's own family. He came out cursing, and he kept on cursing.
16.6: He threw stones at David and at all his servants—even with the warriors flanking David on both sides.
16.7: 'Out! Out!' Shimei was shouting. 'You man of blood! You contemptible nobody!'
16.8: 'The LORD is repaying you for all the blood of Saul's house—the family you replaced! Now the LORD has given the kingdom to your own son Absalom. And here you are, ruined by your own guilt—you man of blood!'
16.9: Abishai son of Zeruiah said: 'Why should this dead dog curse my lord the king? Let me go over and take off his head.'
16.10: The king said: 'What is this to me, sons of Zeruiah? If the LORD has said to him, Curse David—who am I to stop him?'
16.11: 'My own son—my own flesh—wants me dead. How much more this man? Leave him. Let him curse. The LORD has given him this assignment.'
16.12: 'Perhaps the LORD will see the wrong being done to me and repay me with something good in exchange for this cursing.'
16.13: David and his men walked on. Shimei walked along the facing hillside, level with them, still cursing, throwing stones, throwing dust.
16.14: The king and all his people arrived at the Jordan exhausted. They rested there.
16.15: Absalom entered Jerusalem with all Israel, and Ahithophel at his side.
16.16: When Hushai the Archite—David's friend—came to Absalom, he cried out: 'Long live the king! Long live the king!'
16.17: Absalom said: 'Is this how you show loyalty to your friend? Why did you not go with David?'
16.18: Hushai said: 'No. The one the LORD has chosen—and this people, and all Israel—to him I belong. With him I stay.'
16.19: 'And who else should I serve but his son? As I served your father, so I will serve you.'
16.20: Absalom turned to Ahithophel: 'Give your counsel. What should we do?'
16.21: Ahithophel said: 'Sleep with your father's concubines—the ones he left to keep the palace. All Israel will hear that you have become permanently estranged from your father. That will steel the nerves of everyone with you.'
16.22: They erected a tent for Absalom on the palace roof. In the sight of all Israel, Absalom went in to his father's concubines. The public violation was the point.
16.23: In those days Ahithophel's counsel was as authoritative as a word from God himself. That was how both David and Absalom had always treated it.

17.1: Ahithophel said to Absalom: 'Give me twelve thousand men. I will go after David tonight.'
17.2: 'He is weary and demoralized. I will fall on him, throw him into panic, scatter his men—and strike down the king alone.'
17.3: 'Then I bring everyone back to you—like a bride returning to her husband. One man is all you need. The rest of the people will be unharmed.'
17.4: The plan pleased Absalom and every elder of Israel.
17.5: Then Absalom said: 'Call Hushai the Archite. Let us hear him too.'
17.6: When Hushai arrived, Absalom told him what Ahithophel had proposed. 'Shall we act on it? What do you say?'
17.7: Hushai said: 'The counsel Ahithophel has given is wrong—this time it is wrong.'
17.8: 'You know your father and his men. They are warriors, and right now they are like a bear who has lost her cubs in the open field—wild with rage. Your father has fought wars his whole life. He will not camp with the people.'
17.9: 'Right now he has hidden somewhere—a pit, a cave, who knows. When he falls on your men first, and the news goes out—There was a slaughter among Absalom's troops—even the bravest hearts will crack.'
17.10: 'The bravest man—heart of a lion—will melt. All Israel knows who David is. They know his men. One ambush from him and the army dissolves.'
17.11: 'My counsel: gather all Israel to yourself—from Dan to Beersheba, numberless as sand—and go into battle yourself.'
17.12: 'We will come on him in whatever place he is found—like dew falling everywhere at once. Not one of him or his men will be left.'
17.13: 'If he takes refuge in a city, all Israel will bring ropes and drag that city into the ravine—not a stone will be left.'
17.14: Absalom and all the men of Israel said: 'The counsel of Hushai is better.' But it was the LORD who had ordained it—the LORD had decided to frustrate Ahithophel's advice, which was genuinely good, because the LORD intended disaster for Absalom.
17.15: Hushai went at once to Zadok and Abiathar: 'Ahithophel counseled this. I countered with that. Here is what was said.'
17.16: 'Send word to David immediately: Do not spend the night at the desert ford. Cross the Jordan now—or the king and everyone with him will be swallowed up.'
17.17: Jonathan and Ahimaaz were waiting at En-rogel. A servant girl would carry word to them; they would carry it to David. They could not be seen going into the city.
17.18: But a young man saw them and reported to Absalom. The two of them ran—to Bahurim, to the house of a man who had a well in his courtyard. They went down into it.
17.19: The woman of the house spread a covering over the well's mouth and scattered grain on top. Nothing was seen.
17.20: Absalom's men came to the woman: 'Where are Ahimaaz and Jonathan?' She said: 'They crossed the stream.' They searched. They found nothing. They returned to Jerusalem.
17.21: When the coast was clear they climbed out of the well, went to David, and told him: 'Get up. Cross the water now. This is what Ahithophel has planned against you.'
17.22: David rose. All his people rose. They crossed the Jordan. By dawn there was not one left on the near bank.
17.23: When Ahithophel saw that his counsel had been rejected, he saddled his donkey and went home to his city. He put his household in order—the last act of a man who knew his life was finished—and hanged himself. He died and was buried in his father's tomb. His counsel had been sound; it was rejected anyway. That was more than he could bear.
17.24: David reached Mahanaim. Absalom crossed the Jordan with all Israel behind him.
17.25: Absalom had given Amasa command of the army in place of Joab. Amasa was the son of Ithra the Israelite, who had married Abigail daughter of Nahash—the sister of Zeruiah, Joab's mother. A family connection: David's nephew commanding against David.
17.26: Israel and Absalom camped in Gilead.
17.27: When David arrived at Mahanaim, three men came to meet him: Shobi son of Nahash from Rabbah of the Ammonites, Machir son of Ammiel from Lo-debar, and Barzillai the Gileadite from Rogelim.
17.28: They brought beds, basins, clay pots, wheat, barley, flour, parched grain, beans, lentils, honey—
17.29: —and curds and sheep and cheese. 'The people are hungry, exhausted, and thirsty in the wilderness,' they said. So they fed them.

18.1: David counted his men and organized them—commanders of thousands, commanders of hundreds.
18.2: He sent the army out in three columns: one under Joab, one under Abishai son of Zeruiah, one under Ittai the Gittite. The king said: 'I will march with you.'
18.3: But the men said: 'You must not come. If we run, they will not pursue us—only you. If half of us die, no one will care—only you matter. You are worth ten thousand of us. Stay in the city and support us from there.'
18.4: The king said: 'I will do what seems best to you.' He stood by the gate and watched them march out—hundreds, then thousands.
18.5: 'Deal gently with the young man Absalom—for my sake.' The king said it to Joab, to Abishai, to Ittai—and all the people heard. For my sake. They all heard it.
18.6: The army marched out to meet Israel. The battle was fought in the forest of Ephraim.
18.7: Israel was routed by David's men. Twenty thousand fell that day. The defeat was catastrophic.
18.8: The fighting scattered across the whole country—and the forest swallowed more men that day than the sword did. The wilderness that David had fled into became Absalom's executioner.
18.9: Absalom came across David's men while riding a mule. The mule went under a great oak—and Absalom's head caught in the branches. The mule kept going. Absalom hung there between heaven and earth.
18.10: A soldier saw it and reported to Joab: 'I just saw Absalom—hanging in an oak.'
18.11: Joab said: 'You saw him and did nothing? I would have given you ten pieces of silver and a belt for killing him.'
18.12: The man said: 'A thousand pieces of silver would not make me raise my hand against the king's son. We all heard it—we all heard the king tell you and Abishai and Ittai: Protect the young man Absalom, for my sake.'
18.13: 'And if I had done it—taken his life in defiance of the king's direct order—do you think you would have stood by me? Nothing is hidden from the king. You would have left me to answer for it alone.'
18.14: Joab said: 'I will not waste more words with you.' He took three darts and drove them into Absalom's chest—still alive, still hanging in the tree.
18.15: Ten of Joab's armor-bearers closed in around Absalom and struck him until he was dead.
18.16: Joab blew the ram's horn. The troops turned back from pursuing Israel. Joab stopped the chase.
18.17: They took Absalom's body and threw it into a great pit in the forest and heaped a massive pile of stones over him. All Israel scattered, each man to his own home.
18.18: Absalom had erected a pillar for himself in the King's Valley while he was still alive. 'I have no son to carry my name,' he had said—so he built the monument himself. He named it after himself. It is called Absalom's Monument to this day. The monument outlasted its builder by a few hours.
18.19: Ahimaaz son of Zadok said: 'Let me run and bring the king the news—how the LORD has delivered him from his enemies.'
18.20: Joab said: 'Not today. Another day you may carry news—but not today. The king's son is dead.'
18.21: Then Joab said to the Cushite: 'Go—tell the king what you have seen.' The Cushite bowed and ran.
18.22: Ahimaaz said to Joab again: 'Let me run after the Cushite—whatever comes of it.' Joab said: 'Why run, son? There is no reward waiting for you in this news.'
18.23: 'Whatever comes—let me run.' 'Run then,' said Joab. Ahimaaz took the plain road and outran the Cushite.
18.24: David sat between the two gates. The watchman had gone up to the roof above the gate wall. He looked out and saw a man running—alone.
18.25: The watchman called down to the king. The king said: 'If he is alone, he carries news.' The runner kept coming.
18.26: The watchman saw a second man running and called to the porter: 'Another man, running alone.' The king said: 'He brings news too.'
18.27: The watchman said: 'The running of the first one looks like Ahimaaz son of Zadok.' The king said: 'He is a good man. He will bring good news.'
18.28: Ahimaaz cried out: 'All is well!' He bowed with his face to the ground before the king. 'Blessed be the LORD your God,' he said, 'who has delivered up the men who raised their hand against my lord the king!'
18.29: The king said: 'Is the young man Absalom safe?' Ahimaaz said: 'When Joab sent me I saw a great disturbance—but I did not know what it was.'
18.30: The king said: 'Step aside. Stand there.' He stepped aside and stood still.
18.31: Then the Cushite came. 'Good news, my lord the king! The LORD has delivered you today from all who rose against you.'
18.32: The king said: 'Is the young man Absalom safe?' The Cushite answered: 'May all the enemies of my lord the king, all who rise against you to do you harm—may they be as that young man is.'
18.33:
The king staggered. He went up to the chamber above the gate and wept.
My son Absalom—
my son, my son Absalom!
Would I had died instead of you—
O Absalom, my son,
my son!

19.1: Word reached Joab: the king was weeping. Mourning for Absalom.
19.2: The day's victory collapsed into mourning. Word spread through all the army: the king was in grief over his son.
19.3: The troops crept back into the city that day like men who had fled a battle in disgrace—skulking in, not marching.
19.4: The king had covered his face and was crying out with a broken voice: 'Absalom! O Absalom, my son—my son!'
19.5: Joab came in to the king and said bluntly: 'You have shamed every man who saved your life today—your sons, your daughters, your wives, your concubines—
19.6: '—by loving those who hate you and hating those who love you. You have made it plain: your commanders and your men count for nothing. If Absalom were alive and we were all dead today, you would be satisfied.'
19.7: 'Get up. Go out and speak a word of encouragement to your men. I swear by the LORD: if you do not go out, not a single man will be with you by morning. That will be worse for you than every evil that has ever come on you from your earliest days until now.'
19.8: The king got up and took his seat at the city gate. Word went out: the king is at the gate. The people came, every one of them. But all Israel had already scattered—each man to his own tent.
19.9: All across the tribes of Israel, the people were arguing: 'The king is the one who rescued us from our enemies, who saved us from the Philistines. Now he has fled the land because of Absalom.'
19.10: 'And Absalom—the man we anointed as king—is dead. So why is no one saying a word about bringing David back?'
19.11: King David sent word to the priests Zadok and Abiathar: 'Tell the elders of Judah: why should you be the last to bring the king home? All Israel is already talking. Why is Judah slow?'
19.12: 'You are my brothers. My own bone and blood. Why should you be the last to bring your king home?'
19.13: 'And say to Amasa: You are my own flesh and blood. God deal with me as he sees fit if you are not commander of my army from this day on, in Joab's place.'
19.14: He turned the hearts of all the men of Judah—as one man—and they sent to the king: 'Come back, you and all who are with you.'
19.15: The king turned back and came to the Jordan. Judah had assembled at Gilgal to meet him and bring him across.
19.16: Shimei son of Gera the Benjaminite—from Bahurim, the man who had cursed David—came hurrying down with the men of Judah to meet the king.
19.17: A thousand men from Benjamin were with him, and Ziba the steward of Saul's household—his fifteen sons and twenty servants—all crossing the Jordan to get to the king first.
19.18: They crossed the ford to help move the king's household and to serve him in any way he required. Shimei son of Gera threw himself down before the king just as David reached the ford.
19.19: 'My lord the king—do not count it against me. Do not remember what your servant did wrong on the day you left Jerusalem. Let the king put it from his heart.'
19.20: 'I know what I did was sin. But look—today I am the first of all Joseph's house to come down to meet my lord the king.'
19.21: But Abishai son of Zeruiah cut in: 'Should Shimei not die for this? He cursed the LORD's anointed.'
19.22: David said: 'What is it with you sons of Zeruiah? Why do you become my opponents today? Should any man die in Israel today? Do I not know that today I am king over Israel?'
19.23: The king said to Shimei, 'You will not die.' He gave him his word under oath.
19.24: Mephibosheth son of Saul came down to meet the king. He had not trimmed his beard, or washed his clothes, or tended his feet from the day David left until the day he came back safe.
19.25: When he came to meet the king at Jerusalem, David asked him, 'Mephibosheth—why didn't you come with me?'
19.26: 'My lord the king—my servant deceived me. I told him to saddle a donkey so I could ride and go with you. I am lame; I could not go on foot.'
19.27: 'He slandered me to you, my lord. But you are like an angel of God—do what is right in your eyes.'
19.28: 'All my father's house deserved death from you, my lord. Yet you gave me a place at your table. What further claim could I make to the king?'
19.29: 'Enough,' the king said. 'The matter is settled: you and Ziba divide the land.'
19.30: Mephibosheth said, 'Let him take everything—since my lord the king has come home in peace.'
19.31: Barzillai the Gileadite had come down from Rogelim and escorted the king to the Jordan to see him safely across.
19.32: Barzillai was eighty years old—a very old man. He had supplied the king throughout his stay at Mahanaim, being a man of great means.
19.33: 'Come across with me,' the king said to Barzillai. 'I will provide for you in Jerusalem.'
19.34: Barzillai answered: 'How many years do I have left? Why should I go up to Jerusalem with the king?'
19.35: 'I am eighty years old. I can no longer taste food or drink or tell good from bad. I can't even hear the voices of singers. Why should I be a burden to my lord the king?'
19.36: 'I will cross the Jordan with you just a little way. Why should the king recompense me so?'
19.37: 'Let me turn back and die in my own city, near the graves of my father and mother. But here is your servant Chimham—let him go with my lord the king. Do for him whatever seems right to you.'
19.38: The king said, 'Chimham will come with me. I will do for him whatever you ask. And anything you desire from me, I will do.'
19.39: All the people crossed the Jordan; the king crossed over. The king kissed Barzillai and gave him his blessing. Then Barzillai went home.
19.40: The king moved on to Gilgal, Chimham with him. All the people of Judah and half of Israel escorted the king.
19.41: All the men of Israel came to the king with their complaint: 'Why did our brothers of Judah steal you away—bringing the king and his household across the Jordan, and all his men—without us?'
19.42: The men of Judah answered Israel: 'The king is our kinsman. Why are you angry? Have we taken anything from him? Has he given us a single gift?'
19.43: Israel shot back: 'We have ten shares in the king—we have greater claim on David than you do. Why did you despise us? Were we not the first to speak about bringing the king home?' But the men of Judah spoke more harshly than the men of Israel.

20.1: There was a worthless man there named Sheba son of Bichri, a Benjaminite. He blew the horn and called out: 'We have no share in David, no inheritance in Jesse's son! Every man back to his own tent—Israel, go home!'
20.2: All the men of Israel abandoned David and went after Sheba son of Bichri. But the men of Judah stayed loyal to their king from the Jordan all the way to Jerusalem.
20.3: When David reached his palace in Jerusalem, he took the ten concubines he had left behind to keep the household. He placed them in a guarded house and provided for them, but he never again went in to them. They lived there, shut away, for the rest of their lives—as if widowed but not widowed.
20.4: The king ordered Amasa: 'Muster the men of Judah—three days. And be here yourself.'
20.5: Amasa went to muster Judah, but he was late—he missed the deadline.
20.6: David said to Abishai, 'Sheba son of Bichri will do us more damage than Absalom ever did. Take the men and go after him now—before he finds fortified cities to hide in and escapes us.'
20.7: Joab's men went out along with the Cherethites and Pelethites and all the fighting men—out from Jerusalem in pursuit of Sheba son of Bichri.
20.8: They were at the great stone in Gibeon when Amasa came to meet them. Joab was in military dress, with a sword belted at his thigh—and as he moved forward, it slipped from the sheath.
20.9: 'Are you well, brother?' Joab said to Amasa—and grabbed him by the beard with his right hand to give him a kiss.
20.10: Amasa did not see the sword in Joab's hand. Joab drove it into his stomach—and his intestines spilled out on the ground. One blow was enough. He died. Then Joab and Abishai pressed on after Sheba son of Bichri.
20.11: One of Joab's men stood over the body and called out: 'If you are for Joab and for David, follow Joab!'
20.12: Amasa lay in the middle of the road, soaked in his own blood. Every man who came by stopped to look. So Joab's man dragged the body off the road into the field and threw a garment over it—and once it was covered, the men moved on.
20.13: With the body out of sight, all the men followed Joab in the pursuit of Sheba son of Bichri.
20.14: Sheba moved through all the tribes of Israel and came to Abel Beth-maacah. All the Bichrites assembled there and followed him into the city.
20.15: Joab's men came and besieged him at Abel Beth-maacah. They built a siege ramp against the city wall and were pounding it down.
20.16: Then a wise woman called out from the city: 'Listen! Tell Joab to come closer—I want to speak to him.'
20.17: He came near. 'Are you Joab?' she said. 'I am,' he said. 'Then hear what your servant has to say.' 'I am listening,' said Joab.
20.18: 'In the old days,' she said, 'people used to say: go to Abel for an answer. And matters were settled. This city has always been a place of wise counsel.'
20.19: 'I represent the peaceful and faithful of Israel. You are trying to destroy a city that is a mother-city in Israel. Why would you devour the LORD's own inheritance?'
20.20: 'God forbid,' Joab answered. 'Swallow up or destroy? Never.'
20.21: 'Not at all. A man named Sheba son of Bichri, from the hill country of Ephraim, has lifted his hand against King David. Give him to us, and we will leave the city alone.' 'His head will come over the wall to you,' the woman said.
20.22: The wise woman took her counsel to the people of the city. They cut off Sheba's head and threw it over the wall to Joab. He blew the horn, his men dispersed from the city—every man to his own home—and Joab returned to Jerusalem to the king.
20.23: Joab commanded all Israel's army. Benaiah son of Jehoiada commanded the Cherethites and Pelethites.
20.24: Adoram oversaw the forced labor. Jehoshaphat son of Ahilud was the court recorder.
20.25: Sheva was the royal secretary. Zadok and Abiathar were the priests.
20.26: And Ira the Jairite was David's own priest.

21.1: In David's time there was a famine—three years in a row. David sought the LORD, and the LORD told him: 'Bloodguilt rests on Saul and his house, because he put the Gibeonites to death.'
21.2: The king summoned the Gibeonites. They were not Israelites—they were a surviving remnant of the Amorites, a people Israel had sworn to protect. Yet Saul, in his zeal for Israel and Judah, had tried to destroy them.
21.3: David asked the Gibeonites, 'What can I do for you? How can atonement be made, so that you will bless the LORD's inheritance?'
21.4: The Gibeonites said, 'This is not a matter of silver and gold between us and Saul. And it is not for us to put anyone in Israel to death.' David said, 'Whatever you ask, I will do.'
21.5: 'The man who devoured us,' they said, 'the man who planned to exterminate us so that we would have no place left in all of Israel—'
21.6: '—give us seven of his male descendants so we can execute them before the LORD at Gibeah of Saul, the one the LORD chose.' The king said, 'I will give them.'
21.7: The king spared Mephibosheth, Jonathan's son—Saul's grandson—because of the oath sworn before the LORD between David and Jonathan.
21.8: The king took Armoni and Mephibosheth—the two sons Rizpah daughter of Aiah had borne to Saul—along with five sons of Merab daughter of Saul, born to Adriel son of Barzillai the Meholathite.
21.9: He handed them to the Gibeonites, who executed them on the mountain before the LORD. All seven fell together, at the start of the barley harvest—the very first days of harvest time.
21.10: Rizpah daughter of Aiah took sackcloth and spread it on the rock. From the beginning of harvest until the rains came from heaven, she kept her watch—driving the birds away by day and the wild animals away by night.
21.11: Word reached David of what Rizpah daughter of Aiah had done.
21.12: David went and recovered the bones of Saul and his son Jonathan from the men of Jabesh-gilead—men who had taken them secretly from the open square at Beth-shan, where the Philistines had hung them on the day they killed Saul at Gilboa.
21.13: He brought back the bones of Saul and Jonathan, and they gathered the bones of the seven who had been hanged.
21.14: They buried the bones of Saul and Jonathan in Zela of Benjamin, in the tomb of Kish his father. They did everything the king commanded. And after that, God heard the plea for the land—the plague was lifted.
21.15: War broke out again between the Philistines and Israel. David went down with his men to fight, and David grew exhausted in the battle.
21.16: Ishbi-benob—one of the Rephaite giants, carrying a spear with a bronze head weighing three hundred shekels and armed with a new sword—was about to kill David.
21.17: Abishai son of Zeruiah came to his rescue, struck the Philistine down and killed him. Then David's men swore an oath to him: 'You will not go out to battle with us again. You must not extinguish the lamp of Israel.'
21.18: After this another battle was fought against the Philistines at Gob. Sibbecai the Hushathite struck down Saph—one of the Rephaite giants.
21.19: Another battle at Gob with the Philistines: Elhanan son of Jaare-oregim the Bethlehemite struck down Goliath of Gath, whose spear shaft was like a weaver's beam.
21.20: Another battle at Gath—and there was a man of extraordinary size: six fingers on each hand, six toes on each foot—twenty-four in all. He too was of Rephaite descent.
21.21: He taunted Israel, and Jonathan son of Shimei, David's own brother, struck him down.
21.22: These four were descendants of the Rephaite giants of Gath. They all fell at the hands of David and his men.

22.1: David sang this song to the LORD on the day the LORD rescued him from the grip of all his enemies and from the hand of Saul.
22.2:
He said:
The LORD is my rock,
my fortress, my deliverer—
22.3:
the God who is my Rock—in him I hide.
My shield, the horn of my salvation,
my fortress, my refuge,
my savior who rescues me from violence.
22.4:
I call to the LORD—he is worthy of all praise—
and I am saved from my enemies.
22.5:
The waves of death closed over me,
the torrents of chaos swept at me;
22.6:
the cords of Sheol tightened around me,
the snares of death were laid before me.
